# OQS status meeting – 2026-07-14 – summary

*Note: Generated by AI based on the agenda and transcript.*

## Attendees

- Bruce Xu (University of Waterloo)
- Douglas Stebila (University of Waterloo)
- JP Lomas (Die QRL Stiftung)
- Mark Albert
- Michael Baentsch (independent contributor)
- Norman Ashley (Cisco)
- Rodrigo Martín (Indra)

**Date and time:** Tuesday, July 14, 2026, 12:30–13:18 PM US Eastern / 9:30 AM US Pacific / 18:30 Central European

**Location:** Zoom

Information about the OQS project is available at <https://openquantumsafe.org>. The project dashboard is at <https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html>.

## OQS Technical Steering Committee

Rodrigo summarised the past week's TSC activity, most of which flowed from last week's TSC meeting: the vote to establish a new TSC vice chair from September onwards ([PR #315](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/315)) — Rodrigo thanked Douglas for agreeing to take the role — and several items on the security process. On the security-process side, this included a change to stop referencing the security email as part of the security response process ([issue #293](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/293)) and the creation of the new private security-tracking repository ([PR #313](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/313)), which lets the team map security advisories and issues into a single repository in an automated way. As part of that effort, Bruce uploaded the Trail of Bits analysis reports and Rodrigo mapped them to specific issues, which are now all created in the repository. No further discussion was raised on the TSC.

## liboqs

### liboqs 0.16.0 release

Rodrigo congratulated the team, and Bruce in particular, on the release of liboqs 0.16.0. Post-release, some follow-up activity has appeared (e.g. comments on the External-µ and ACVP items), but no specific issue or PR was flagged for urgent attention beyond the backlog discussion below.

### Open PR backlog and prioritisation

Bruce noted a large backlog of open PRs (20+) that he plans to work through this week and next, held up until now by the 0.16 release rather than by any inherent difficulty. His initial approach was first-come, first-served; Michael and Rodrigo both pushed for some explicit rationale/prioritisation instead (e.g. assigning PRs to a milestone). Rodrigo observed that the biggest 0.17 milestone item — the round-3 onramp algorithms — is not yet reflected in open PRs, and that among the current PRs no single one obviously merits priority. Bruce characterised much of the backlog as test-suite, CI/CD, and documentation enhancements that mostly just need code review. Douglas noted that the small PRs can be chipped away at in spare time, while larger ones (e.g. [PR #2476](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2476), derandomized keypair generation for ML-DSA) need API-design consideration and back-and-forth with contributors, and should be started early to absorb the round-trip latency.

Rodrigo raised the broader tension between reducing the backlog and preparing over the summer for round-3 inclusion (documentation of the integration process, copy-from-upstream / OQS builder improvements — Basil's work). Bruce proposed roughly a 60/40 split between new round-3 preparation work and backlog reduction, and suggested that the summer preparation be captured as explicit issues mapped to the 0.17 milestone, giving the team a clear "finish line" for the release — though he did not yet have a concrete list and needs to review the copy-from-upstream tooling first. On round-3 timing, Rodrigo noted OQS is still missing 5 of the 9 algorithms: SQIsign (Basil already working on it), plus support signalled from several others (e.g. UOV), with one submission he expects will not get author support and two still unknown. Norman suggested not pressing hard on the backlog just yet — give it a couple of weeks and revisit if Bruce needs help.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce):** Work through the liboqs open-PR backlog over the coming week (targeting a manageable number by next Tuesday), splitting time roughly 60/40 between round-3 preparation and backlog reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce):** Review the copy-from-upstream / OQS builder tooling and produce an explicit list of round-3 preparation tasks, captured as issues mapped to the 0.17 milestone.

### Distributing the load

Michael pushed back that too much of the work is landing on Bruce's shoulders, with no one else volunteering to take load off, and that this differs from how the project has run before. Bruce welcomed a second review on already-reviewed PRs that have gone stale — specifically calling out the ML-DSA External-µ PR (missing a second review) and the Markdown documentation linting ([PR #2446](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2446), an unfortunate cut from 0.16) — and invited others to pick and choose PRs of interest, noting the project deliberately does not run a formal Scrum/Agile assignment process. Rodrigo took Michael's comment to heart and encouraged everyone, himself included, to help review PRs and answer incoming issues. Douglas offered to work with Bruce on prioritisation/tagging and on putting together a project board for 0.17 to flag where contributor help is wanted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Volunteer to take a second review on open liboqs PRs, especially already-reviewed ones that have gone stale (e.g. ML-DSA External-µ [#2366](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2366), Markdown documentation linting [#2446](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2446)).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas, Bruce):** Work together on prioritising/tagging liboqs PRs and set up a 0.17 project board to organise the work and flag where contributor help is wanted.

### Architectural decision records

Following up on last week's discussion, Michael again asked Douglas to write down a few architectural decision records (ADRs) — as Michael did for oqs-provider with Claude's help — to capture design rationale so that prioritisation and scope decisions don't fall solely on Douglas and so others understand how things fit together. Douglas agreed and asked Michael to send example material to get started.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Draft a few architectural decision records (ADRs) capturing key liboqs design decisions to help distribute prioritisation and context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Michael):** Send Douglas his example oqs-provider ADR material as a starting template.

### Google collaboration proposal

Rodrigo noted that he and Douglas (with Claude's help) have drafted a proposal for the previously-discussed collaboration with Google, part of which seeks resources to help with the growing PR backlog and review load.

### Classic McEliece (pre-ISO) support — issue [#2490](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2490)

Rodrigo raised, for awareness, that ISO has standardised both ML-KEM and Classic McEliece, and that on [issue #2490](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2490) Logan (maintainer of the CAICA implementation) volunteered to support the *pre-ISO* Classic McEliece implementation currently held in liboqs. Rodrigo argued (as a personal opinion) against continuing to hold that pre-ISO implementation: unlike Kyber, this variant was never selected by NIST for standardisation, and ISO standardised a *different* implementation — so it is effectively an experimental algorithm in the form OQS holds it. The current liboqs implementation derives from PQClean, whose maintainers have previously been unwilling to help integrate the standardised version. Norman countered that if someone is willing to support an algorithm and it is being used, keeping it helps grow the contributor pool. Michael tied the disagreement back to the need for ADRs / documented inclusion criteria: what is OQS meant to support — algorithms the core team understands, algorithms someone uses, or algorithms someone outside is willing to maintain? Douglas cautioned that the project's original intent need not define its purpose forever. All agreed this is a mission-level question best resolved at the TSC meeting, where the criteria for algorithm inclusion should be made explicit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (TSC):** Discuss at the next TSC meeting whether to continue supporting the pre-ISO Classic McEliece implementation ([issue #2490](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2490)), and more broadly make explicit the criteria/conditions for an algorithm to be included in OQS.

### Security issues and possible API changes

Norman reported that Cisco has been independently assessing security issues in the liboqs codebase and, now that the Trail of Bits report is available, will compare it against Cisco's findings and aims to contribute fixes back to liboqs (timing to be determined; Cisco cannot yet share its own findings). He raised whether the recurring class of issues — buffer overflows, over-reads, overwrites — warrants API changes such as adding length parameters to allow bounds checking. Douglas noted a parallel discussion in the PQCP mlkem-native/mldsa-native projects that went the *other* direction — proposing to *remove* length fields, since once the algorithm is fixed the lengths are fixed — and observed that many of the reported liboqs length-field issues are oversold, because the standard entry points route through trusted constants and so the dangerous conditions are not reachable via the specified API (though not every path is safe). Rodrigo and Norman agreed that an API change would be a big deal, especially now, and should only be considered for a genuinely serious issue with no comparable alternative fix. Rodrigo added that he still needs to read the Trail of Bits findings closely before forming an opinion on how to respond, and suggested the team watch for trends across the reported issues.

## OQS-OpenSSL 3 provider

Rodrigo reported the recent provider activity: the encoder-hotpath merge ([PR #778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778)), the documentation clarification on which algorithms are disabled on OpenSSL ≥ 3.5 ([PR #790](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/790)), and the TLS signature-algorithm propagation fix. His own weekly-CI PR ([PR #795](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/795)) is still pending merge.

The main item was reintroducing milestone-based planning for the provider. Following last week's question about whether to cut a provider release matching liboqs 0.16, and the observation that there was no explicit list of what a release should contain, Rodrigo has created milestones and populated them with issues he thinks belong in the next (0.12) release and the one after. He asked everyone who works on the provider to review the pending issues/PRs and chime in on what they consider necessary for the release. Douglas endorsed the milestone approach and confirmed this partially answers his question about a matching provider release. Rodrigo argued the 0.12 provider release is needed reasonably soon because 0.16 liboqs users need the corresponding provider (e.g. FrodoKEM changes, HQC reactivation) and would otherwise have to run against master; he aims to land it in the next couple of weeks and will keep pressing on it. Michael had previously flagged three issues he wants covered before the release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all provider contributors):** Review the newly-created oqs-provider milestones/issues and comment on what is necessary for the next (0.12) provider release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Drive the oqs-provider 0.12 release (matching liboqs 0.16), targeted for the next couple of weeks, including the three issues Michael flagged as release blockers.

## Language wrappers

### liboqs-Python

Douglas has prepared a release matching liboqs 0.16.0 on a branch and invited anyone interested to review it; otherwise he will finalise and tag it as the next liboqs-Python release in the next couple of days. Consistent with practice, the wrapper version will match the corresponding liboqs version (0.16).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Finalise and tag the liboqs-Python 0.16 release in the next couple of days.

### liboqs-Go

JP flagged that liboqs-Go needs a retrospective release: the last tagged release was 0.12 back in January 2025 (roughly a year and a half ago), leaving unreleased changes on `main` ([issue #53](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/53)). JP proposed, more broadly, tying wrapper releases to liboqs releases — automatically triggered from the liboqs release CI, with matching version numbers, so consumers of a given liboqs-Go version get the corresponding liboqs features. Douglas was supportive but cautious: he was unsure the team has the discipline to reliably keep wrappers clean and ready ahead of each liboqs release, and stressed that a wrapper must never delay a liboqs release. JP addressed this by proposing that a triggered wrapper-release workflow which fails would surface the failure *within the wrapper* (needing remediation and re-triggering there), without blocking or appearing as a failure in the liboqs release. The group agreed JP should first write up the proposed process as a GitHub discussion (rather than a formal document or code) so it can be reviewed — with Douglas pointing out any gaps from his knowledge of the overall project — before any implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce / maintainers):** Tag and publish the missing retrospective liboqs-Go release ([issue #53](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/53)) to cover the changes on `main` since the January 2025 (0.12) release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (JP Lomas):** Open a GitHub discussion proposing a triggered, version-matched automatic release workflow for the language wrappers (surfacing failures within each wrapper without blocking the liboqs release), for team review before implementation.

### liboqs-Rust

Some activity noted, with no specific items raised for discussion.

Rodrigo noted an intent (tracked in a TSC issue) to include the wrappers in the scope of the quarterly project health report, since the wrappers are an interesting part of the project that has seen a lack of maintenance and support in recent months.
